Question:
Given below are the two statements: One is labelled as assertion (A) and other is labelled as reason (R).
Assertion (A): A mixture containing two enantiomers in equal proportions will have zero optical rotation.
Reason (R): In a racemic mixture, the rotation due to one isomer will be cancelled by the rotation due to other isomer.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
Options: Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is NOT the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Solution: A racemic mixture contains two enantiomers in equal proportions due to which optical rotation of one isomer will be cancelled by the rotation due to other isomer. Hence, resultant optical rotation of the mixture becomes zero.

Question: If the sum of the two unit vectors is also a unit vector, then magnitude of their difference is
Solution:
At 120°
The resultant of 2 equal forces are equal to a force of same magnitude.
$\begin{gathered}
  \left| {\vec A} \right| = 1 \\
  \left| {\vec B} \right| = 1 \\
  \left| {\vec A + \vec B} \right| = 1\;\;({\text{given}}) \\
\end{gathered} $

Hence,
$\vec A - \vec B$

$\left| {\vec A - \vec B} \right| = \sqrt {{A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos 60^\circ } $
$\sqrt {1 + 1 + 2\cos 60^\circ } = \sqrt 3 $

Question: Which of the following compounds is optically active due to the absence of both a plane of symmetry (POS) and a center of symmetry (COS)?
Solution:
(1) meso-Tartaric acid
Contains a plane of symmetry.
Therefore, it is achiral and optically inactive.
(2) trans-1,4-Dichlorocyclohexane
Possesses a center of symmetry in its most stable conformation.
Hence, it is achiral.
(3) cis-1,3-Dimethylcyclohexane
Contains a plane of symmetry.
Therefore, it is achiral.
(4) trans-1,2-Dimethylcyclobutane
Does not possess a plane of symmetry.
Does not possess a center of symmetry.
Exists as a pair of enantiomers.
Therefore, it is optically active.

Question: A box containing N molecules of a perfect gas at temperature ${T_1}$, and pressure ${P_1}$. The number of molecules in the box is doubled keeping the total kinetic energy of the gas same as before. If the new pressure is ${P_2}$, and temperature ${T_2}$, then
Solution:
\[{E_{Total}} = \frac{f}{2}NkT \Rightarrow T \propto \frac{1}{N}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left[ {{E_{{\rm{total}}}},f,k = {\rm{constant}}} \right]\]

$$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{T_2}}}{{{T_1}}} = \frac{{{N_1}}}{{{N_2}}} = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow {T_2} = \frac{{{T_1}}}{2}$$

Also from $$PV = NkT \Rightarrow P \propto NT$$

$$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{P_1}}}{{{P_2}}} = \frac{{{N_1}}}{{{N_2}}}.\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{1} = \frac{1}{1}.$$

Question: 20 g of ice at ${{0}^{\circ }}\text{C}$ is mixed with 80 g of water at ${{40}^{\circ }}\text{C}$ in a perfectly insulated calorimeter. The specific heat of water is $1\text{cal}/{{\text{g}}^{\circ }}\text{C}$ and the latent heat of fusion of ice is $80\text{cal}/\text{g}$. The final equilibrium temperature of the mixture will be:
Solution: The heat required to completely melt the ice is ${{Q}_{\text{melt }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}={{m}_{\text{ice }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}\times {{L}_{f}}=20\times 80=1600$ cal. The maximum heat that can be released by the water cooling down to ${{0}^{\circ }}\text{C}$ is ${{Q}_{\text{cool }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}={{m}_{\text{water }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}\times c\times \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }T=80\times 1\times 40=$ 3200 cal. Since ${{Q}_{\text{cool }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}>{{Q}_{\text{melt }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$, all the ice will melt. The remaining heat $\left( 3200-1600=1600\text{cal} \right)$ will then raise the temperature of the total 100 g of water. Using $Q=mc\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }T$, we get $1600=100\times 1\times \left( T-0 \right)$, which yields $T={{16}^{\circ }}\text{C}$.

Question: All of the following are correct w.r.t mycorrhiza, except:
Options: Helps plants in absorption of phosphorus from the soil
Mycorrhiza is a parasitic association between fungi and plants
Provides tolerance to salinity and drought in plants
Solution: Fungi are also known to form symbiotic associations with plants (mycorrhiza). Many members of the genus Glomus form mycorrhiza. The fungal symbiont in these associations absorbs phosphorus from soil and passes it to the plant. Plants having such associations show other benefits also, such as resistance to root-borne pathogens, tolerance to salinity and drought.

Question: A 15 g mass of nitrogen gas is enclosed in a vessel at a temperature $27^\circ C$. Amount of heat transferred to the gas, so that rms velocity of molecules is doubled is about (Take $R = 8.3J/K - mol$ )
Solution:
We know that from kinetic theory of gases,
${V_{rms}} = \sqrt {\frac{{3RT}}{M}} \,{\text{or}}\,{V_{rms}} \propto \sqrt T $
where, R is gas constant, T is temperature and M is molecular mass of a gas.
Here, to double the ${v_{rms}},$ temperature must 4 times of the initial temperature.
$\therefore $ New temperature,
$\begin{gathered}
  {T_2} = 4 \times {T_1} \\
  {\text{As}},\,\,{T_1} = 27^\circ C = 300K \\
\end{gathered} $
$\therefore {T_2} = 4 \times 300 = 1200\,K$
As, gas is kept inside a closed vessel.
$\begin{gathered}
  \therefore Q = n{C_v}.dT \\
   = \frac{{15}}{{28}} \times \frac{{5R}}{2}\left( {1200 - 300} \right) \\
  \left[ {{\text{As}},n = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{{15}}{{28}}{\text{for}}\,{\text{nitrogen}}} \right] \\
\end{gathered} $
$\begin{gathered}
   = \frac{{15}}{{28}} \times \frac{5}{2} \times 8.3 \times 900 \\
  \left[ {{\text{Given}},\,R = 8.3J/K\, - {\text{mole}}} \right] \\
\end{gathered} $
or Q = 10004.4 J = 10kJ

Question:
Match the inter-relations of elastic constants in List I with the corresponding physical quantities in List II.

A. $\frac{Y}{3\left( 1-2\sigma \right)}$I.Modulus of Rigidity $\left( \eta \right)$

Question: Propanoic acid is treated with Br₂ in the presence of red phosphorus (P/Br₂). The product obtained is then treated with excess NH₃. What is the final product?
Solution:
Step 1: Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVZ) Reaction
Propanoic acid reacts with Br₂/red phosphorus and undergoes α-bromination.
CH₃CH₂COOH → CH₃CHBrCOOH
Product formed: 2-Bromopropanoic acid
(Bromine always enters α-carbon adjacent to –COOH group)

Step 2: Reaction with Excess NH₃
The bromine atom is substituted by NH₂ group via nucleophilic substitution.
CH₃CHBrCOOH + NH₃ → CH₃CH(NH₂)COOH
Final product: 2-Aminopropanoic acid (Alanine)
Final Answer: (b)

Question: A hypothetical nucleus $X$ has a mass number $A$ and a nuclear radius $R$. If the nucleons are rearranged into a new spherical configuration $Y$ such that the average distance between nucleons is halved, the density of the new configuration $Y$ relative to the original nuclear density $\rho $ will be:
Solution: Nuclear density is $\rho =$ Mass/Volume. If the average distance between nucleons is halved, the linear dimensions (radius) of the configuration for the same number of nucleons $A$ will decrease by a factor of 2 ( ${R}'=R/2$). Volume $V\propto {{R}^{3}}$, so the new volume ${V}'=V/8$. Since mass remains constant, ${\rho }'=$ Mass $/\left( V/8 \right)=8($Mass $/V)=8\rho $.

Question: Assuming Newton’s law of cooling to be valid. The temperature of body changes from 60⁰ C to 40⁰ C in 7 minutes. Temperature of surroundings being 10⁰ C. Find its temperature after next 7 minutes.
Solution:
Let after next 7 min, its temperature be θ. From Newton's law of cooling
\[\frac{{{\theta _1} - {\theta _2}}}{t} \propto \left( {\frac{{{\theta _1} + {\theta _2}}}{2} - {\theta _0}} \right)\]
Where θ0 = temperature of surrounding.
\[\therefore \frac{{60 - 40}}{7} \propto \left( {\frac{{60 + 40}}{2} - 10} \right)\] -(i)
and \[\frac{{40 - \theta }}{7} \propto \left( {\frac{{40 + \theta }}{2} - 10} \right)\] -(ii)
Dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we obtain
\[\frac{{20}}{7} \times \frac{7}{{40 - \theta }} = \frac{{40}}{{\left( {20 + \theta } \right)/2}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{20}}{{40 - \theta }} = \frac{{40 \times 2}}{{20 + \theta }}\]
\[ \Rightarrow 20 + \theta  = 160 - 4\theta \]
\[ \Rightarrow 5\theta  = 160 - 20 = 140\]
\[\therefore \theta  = \frac{{140}}{5} = {28^0}C\]

Question: A diamagnetic material in a magnetic field moves
Options: from stronger to the weaker parts of the field
from weaker to the stronger parts of the field
in none of the above directions
Solution: When a diamagnetic material is subjected to a magnetic field, it experiences a force due to the induced magnetic moment. This force minimize the magnetic field inside the material and Diamagnetic materials are repelled by magnetic field.

Question:
A copper wire (Y = 1011 N/m2) of length 4 m and steel wire (Y = 2 × 1011 N/m2) of length 2 m each 0.25 cm2 cross-section are fastened end to end and stretched with a tension of 500 Ν.
Based on the given information match entries in column I with its correct entries in column II.
Column-IColumn-II
P.Elongation in copper wire (i)0.6 mm
Q.Elongation in steel wire(ii)1.0 mm
R.Total elongation(iii)0.8 mm
S.Difference in the elongation(iv)0.2 mm
Solution:
We know, \[Y = \frac{{FI}}{{Ax}}\]
\[\therefore \;{X_{Cu}} = \frac{{F{I_{Cu}}}}{{A{Y_{Cu}}}} = \frac{{\left( {500} \right)\left( 4 \right)}}{{\left( {0.25 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}} \right)\left( {{{10}^{11}}} \right)}} = 0.8\;{\text{mm}}\]
Similarly,
\[{X_{steel}} = \frac{{F{I_{steel}}}}{{A{Y_{steel}}}} = \frac{{500\left( 2 \right)}}{{\left( {0.25 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}} \right)\left( {2 \times {{10}^{11}}} \right)}}\]=0.2 mm
∴ Total elongation = 0.8 + 0.2 = 1 mm

Question: A composite rod is constructed by joining two rods of different materials end-to-end. The initial lengths of the rods are ${{L}_{1}}$ and ${{L}_{2}}$, and their respective coefficients of linear expansion are ${{\alpha }_{1}}$ and ${{\alpha }_{2}}$. The effective coefficient of linear expansion of the composite rod is:
Solution: The total elongation $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }L$ of the composite rod for a temperature change $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }T$ is the sum of individual elongations: $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }L=\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{L}_{1}}+\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{L}_{2}}={{\alpha }_{1}}{{L}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }T+{{\alpha }_{2}}{{L}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }T$. By definition, the effective linear expansion coefficient ${{\alpha }_{\text{eff }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ satisfies $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }L={{\alpha }_{\text{eff }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}\left( {{L}_{1}}+{{L}_{2}} \right)\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }T$. Equating these expressions yields ${{\alpha }_{\text{eff }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{{{\alpha }_{1}}{{L}_{1}}+{{\alpha }_{2}}{{L}_{2}}}{{{L}_{1}}+{{L}_{2}}}$.

Question: A metallic cube has a mass of 5.580 kg and each side measures 9.0 cm at ${{20}^{\circ }}\text{C}$. If the coefficient of linear expansion of the metal is $\alpha =2.0\times {{10}^{-5}}{{~}^{\circ }}{{\text{C}}^{-1}}$, find the density of the material at ${{120}^{\circ }}\text{C}$, keeping significant figures in view. Express the result as $X\times {{10}^{3}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ kg }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}^{-3}}$.
Solution: At ${{20}^{\circ }}\text{C}$, Volume ${{V}_{0}}={{(0.090)}^{3}}=7.29\times {{10}^{-4}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}^{3}}$. At $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }T={{100}^{\circ }}\text{C},$ $V={{V}_{0}}\left( 1+3\alpha \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }T \right)=7.29\times {{10}^{-4}}\left( 1+0.006 \right)=7.333\times {{10}^{-4}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}^{3}}$. Density $\rho =\frac{5.580}{7.333\times {{10}^{-4}}}\approx 7609\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ kg }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}^{-3}}$. Since the side 9.0 cm has two significant figures, the result must be rounded to two: $7.6\times {{10}^{3}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ kg }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}^{-3}}$.

Question: The binding energies per nucleon of an isotope X (mass number 4) and an isotope Y (mass number 8) are 2.5 MeV and 8.0 MeV , respectively. If two nuclei of X undergo nuclear fusion to form a single nucleus of Y , the energy released in this reaction is:
Solution: The total binding energy of the reactants (two X nuclei) is ${{E}_{1}}=2\times (4$ nucleons $\times 2.5\text{MeV}/$ nucleon $)=20\text{MeV}$. The total binding energy of the product (one Y nucleus) is ${{E}_{2}}=8$ nucleons $\times 8.0\text{MeV}/$ nucleon $=$ 64 MeV . The energy released during fusion is the difference in the total binding energies: $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }E={{E}_{2}}-{{E}_{1}}=64\text{MeV}-20\text{MeV}=44\text{MeV}$.

Question: Least count of a vernier calliper is \[\frac{1}{{20\,N}}\] cm. The value of one division on the main scale is 1 mm. Then the number of divisions of main scale that coincide with N divisions of vernier scale is:
Solution:
Least count of vernier calipers
\[ = \frac{1}{{20\,\,N}}\,{\text{cm}}\]
Least count = 1 MSD – 1 VSD
Let x no. of divisions of main scale coincide with N division of vernier scale, then
\[1\,\,VSD = \frac{{x \times 1\,\,{\text{mm}}}}{N}\]
\[\therefore \frac{1}{{20\,N}}\,{\text{cm}} = 1\,\,{\text{mm}} - \frac{{x \times 1\,\,{\text{mm}}}}{N}\,\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{2N}}{\text{mm}} = 1\,\,{\text{mm}} - \frac{x}{N}\,{\text{mm}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow x = \frac{{2N - 1}}{2}\]

Question: Three identical heat-conducting rods are connected in series. The rods on the sides have thermal conductivity $K$, while the one in the middle has thermal conductivity $3K$. The left end is maintained at temperature ${T_A} = 2T$ and the right end at ${T_B} = T$. In the steady state, the temperature at the left junction is ${T_1}$ and that at the right junction is ${T_2}$. The ratio $\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}}$ is:
Solution:
In steady state, the rate of heat flow $H$ is constant:

$H \propto {K_1}\left( {{T_A} - {T_1}} \right) = {K_2}\left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right) = {K_3}\left( {{T_2} - {T_B}} \right)$
$K\left( {2T - {T_1}} \right) = 3K\left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right) = K\left( {{T_2} - T} \right)$
From ${{\mathbf{K}}_1}$ and ${{\mathbf{K}}_3}:K\left( {2T - {T_1}} \right)$
$ = K\left( {{T_2} - T} \right)2T - {T_1} = {T_2} - T$
$ \Rightarrow {T_1} + {T_2} = 3T \Rightarrow {T_1} = 3T - {T_2}$
From ${{\mathbf{K}}_2}$ and ${{\mathbf{K}}_3}:3K\left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right)$
$ = K\left( {{T_2} - T} \right)3{T_1} - 3{T_2}$
$ = {T_2} - T \Rightarrow 3{T_1} - 4{T_2} = - T$
Solve for ${{\mathbf{T}}_2}$: Substitute ${T_1} = 3T - {T_2}$ into the second equation:
$3\left( {3T - {T_2}} \right) - 4{T_2} = - T9T - 3{T_2} - 4{T_2} = - T$$ \Rightarrow 10T = 7{T_2} \Rightarrow {T_2} = \frac{{10}}{7}T$
Solve for ${{\mathbf{T}}_1}:{T_1} = 3T - {T_2} = 3T - \frac{{10}}{7}T = \frac{{11}}{7}T$
Ratio $\frac{{{{\mathbf{T}}_1}}}{{{{\mathbf{T}}_2}}}:\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}} = \frac{{\frac{{11}}{7}T}}{{\frac{{10}}{7}T}} = \frac{{11}}{{10}}$

Question:
Read the following statements carefully:
I. In a typical eukaryotic cell, the nucleoplasm contains the nucleolus and chromatin, and the nucleolus is the site for active ribosomal RNA synthesis
II. During crossing over, the enzyme involved in the recombination of genetic material is called ligase
III. In sickle-cell anaemia, the substitution of amino acid is from Glutamic acid to Valine at the sixth position of the beta globin chain
IV. In DNA fingerprinting, the VNTR belongs to a class of satellite DNA referred to as mini-satellite
Options: Statements I, II and III are correct
Statements I, II, III and IV are incorrect
Statements I, III and IV are correct
Statements II, III and IV are correct
Solution: Statement II is incorrect because the enzyme primarily responsible for crossing over (recombination) is recombinase. Statements I (Cell), III (Principles of Inheritance), and IV (Molecular Basis) are correct.

Question: How many six-membered carbon rings and five-membered carbon rings are present in a molecule of Buckminsterfullerene (${{\text{C}}_{60}}$)?
Solution: Buckminsterfullerene (${{\text{C}}_{60}}$) features a truncated icosahedron structure resembling a soccer ball. It is uniquely composed of exactly 20 six-membered carbon rings (hexagons) and 12 five-membered carbon rings (pentagons).

Question:
Assertion: 22.4 L of He gas is having NA atoms of He at 1 atm. & 273 K.
Reason: 22.4 L of any gas at 1 atm. & 273 K is having NA atoms.
Options: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect.
Solution:
Statement I claims that 22.4 L of helium gas contains NA atoms of helium. Since 1 mole of any gas contains NA (Avogadro's number) particles, and 1 mole of helium occupies 22.4 L at STP, this statement is correct.
Statement II states that 1 mole of any gas occupies 22.4 L at 1 atm and 273 K. This is a fundamental principle of gas laws and is also correct.

Question: If 250 MeV energy is released in the fission of a single nucleus of \[_z^AX,\] the number of fissions per second which are required to produce a power of 2 kW is
Solution:
Let n is number of fissions per second
Energy released per second = n × 250 MeV
= n × 250 × 1.6 × 10–13 J
Energy required per second = Power × Time
= 2kW × 1s = 2000 J
n × 250 × 1.6 × 10–13 = 2000
\[n = \frac{{2000}}{{400 \times {{10}^{ - 13}}}} = 5 \times {10^{13}}\]

Question: A cylinder of radius R is surrounded by a cylindrical shell of inner radius R and outer radius 3R. The thermal conductivity of the material of the inner cylinder is K<sub>1</sub> and that of the outer cylinder K<sub>2</sub>. Assuming no loss of heat from cylindrical surface, the effective thermal conductivity of the system for heat flowing along the length of the cylinder is ______.

An ideal gas undergoes a cyclic process as shown in Figure.

\[\Delta {U_{BC}}\; = {\rm{ }} - {\rm{ }}5{\rm{ }}kJ{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - \;}}^1,{\rm{ }}{{\rm{Q}}_{AB}}_\; = {\rm{ }}2{\rm{ }}kJ{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - \;}}^1\]
\[{W_{AB}}\; = \;{\rm{ }}5{\rm{ }}kJ{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - \;}}^1,{\rm{ }}{W_{CA}}\; = {\rm{ }} - 3{\rm{ }}kJ{\rm{ }}mo{l^{ - \;}}^1\]
Heat absorbed by the system during process CA is:
Solution:
$$AB \to {\text{isobaric}}$$

$$BC \to {\text{isochoric}}$$

$$CA \to {\text{not}}\,{\text{defined}}$$

For AB

\[Q = \Delta U + W\]                 (First law of thermodynamics)

\[\Delta U_{AB} = 2 - 5 =  - 3\,kJ/mol\]

For ABC

$$\Delta {U_{ABC}} = \Delta {U_{AB}} + \Delta {U_{BC}}$$

\[ = {\rm{ }} - 3{\rm{ }} - {\rm{ }}5{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }} - 8{\rm{ }}kJ/mol\]

For cyclic process $\Delta U = 0$

\[\Delta {U_{ABC}}+\Delta {U_{CA}} =0\\\Rightarrow \Delta U_{CA}= + 8\,kJ/mol\]

\[\Delta U_{CA} = {\rm{ }}Q_{CA}{\rm{  -  }}W_{CA}\]

\[8{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }}Q_{CA}{\rm{ }} + {\rm{ }}3\]

\[Q_{CA}{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }} + 5{\rm{ kJ/mol}}\]

Question: A uniform steel wire of density 7800 kg/ m3 is 2.5 m long and has mass 15.6×10–3 kg. It extends by 1.25 mm when loaded by 8kg. Calculate the value of Young’s modulus of elasticity for steel.
Solution:
Volume = Mass / density
Area of cross – section = volume / length
\[\begin{gathered}
   = \frac{{mass}}{{density \times length}} = \frac{{15.6 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{7800 \times 2.5}} = 8 \times {10^{ - 7}}{m^2} \\
  Y = \frac{{F\ell }}{{A\Delta L}} = \frac{{8 \times 9.8 \times 2.5}}{{\left( {8 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}} \right) \times 1.25 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} \\
  Y = 1.96 \times {10^{11}}N/{m^2} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: An air bubble of volume \[{\text{1}}\,{\text{c}}{{\text{m}}^3}\] rises from the bottom of a lake 40 m deep to the surface at a temperature of ${12^ \circ }{\text{C}}$. The atmospheric pressure is $1 \times {10^5}{\text{Pa}}$, the density of water is $1000{\text{kg/}}{{\text{m}}^{\text{3}}}$ and ${\text{g = 10m/}}{{\text{s}}^{\text{2}}}$. There is no difference of the temperature of water at the depth of 40 m and on the surface. The volume of air bubble when it reaches the surface will be:
Solution:
${\text{P = }}{{\text{P}}_{\text{0}}}{{ + \rho gh}} = {10^5}{\text{Pa}} + {10^3} \times 10 \times 40$
$ = 5 \times {10^5}{\text{Pa}}$
At T is constant
${\text{PV = }}{{\text{P}}_{\text{0}}}{{\text{V}}_{\text{0}}}$
$ \Rightarrow 5 \times {10^5}{\text{Pa}} \times {\text{1c}}{{\text{m}}^3} = {10^5}{{Pa \times }}{{\text{V}}_0}$
$ \Rightarrow {{\text{V}}_{\text{0}}}{\text{ = 5c}}{{\text{m}}^{\text{3}}}$

Question: The nuclear radius of an unknown stable nucleus $X$ is measured to be exactly twice the nuclear radius of a Carbon- $12\left( ~_{6}^{12}\text{C} \right)$ nucleus. Assuming that the empirical relationship for nuclear radius $R={{R}_{0}}{{A}^{1/3}}$ holds true, the mass number $A$ of this unknown nucleus is:
Solution:
According to the empirical relation for spherical nuclei, $R\propto {{A}^{1/3}}$. Writing the ratio for nucleus $X$ and Carbon-12:
$\frac{{{R}_{X}}}{{{R}_{C}}}={{\left( \frac{{{A}_{X}}}{{{A}_{C}}} \right)}^{1/3}}$
Given that ${{R}_{X}}=2{{R}_{C}}$ and ${{A}_{C}}=12$ :
$2={{\left( \frac{{{A}_{X}}}{12} \right)}^{1/3}}$
Cubing both sides of the equation:
$8=\frac{{{A}_{X}}}{12}\Rightarrow {{A}_{X}}=8\times 12=96$

Question: Which of the following statements correctly describes the comparative properties of radioactive emissions?
Options: Alpha particles have the highest penetrating power but the lowest ionizing power.
Beta particles are uncharged and therefore unaffected by external magnetic fields.
Gamma rays possess the highest penetrating power and the lowest ionizing power among the three.
Gamma rays have higher ionizing power than both alpha and beta particles due to their high frequency.
Solution: Gamma rays $\left( \gamma  \right)$ are high-energy electromagnetic photons with zero rest mass and zero charge, giving them the highest penetrating power. However, due to their lack of charge and minimal interaction with matter, they have the lowest ionizing power compared to massive and charged $\alpha $ and $\beta $ particles.

Question: Two wires of equal cross-section but one made of steel and the other of copper are joined end to end. When at the combination is kept under tension, the elongations in the two wires are found to be equal. What is the ratio of the lengths of the two wires lengths (Given, Y for steel = 2 × 1011 Nm-2, Y for copper =  1.1 × 1011 Nm-2  )
Solution:
As, $$Y = \frac{{stress}}{{strain}}$$

Or      $$strain = \frac{{stress}}{Y}$$

Or      $$\frac{{\Delta L}}{L} = \frac{{stress}}{Y}$$

Since, cross-section are equal and same tension exists in both wires, therefore, the stresses developed are equal. Also, $$\Delta L$$ is given to be the same for both the wires.

$$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,L \propto Y$$

$$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\frac{{{L_s}}}{{{L_{{C_u}}}}} = \frac{{{Y_s}}}{{{Y_{{C_u}}}}} = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{11}}}}{{1.1 \times {{10}^{11}}}} = \frac{{20}}{{11}}$$

Question: The power emitted per unit area of a black body is R W/m<sup>2</sup>. At what wavelength will the power radiated by the black body be maximum? The Stefan’s constant is σ  and Wien’s constant is b.
Solution:
$R = \sigma {T^4}$
$\lambda = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{{b{{\left( \sigma \right)}^{1/4}}}}{{{{\left( R \right)}^{1/4}}}}$

Question: Which of the following serves as the main source of biofertilizers in aquatic ecosystems like paddy fields?
Solution: Cyanobacteria (like Anabaena, Nostoc, Oscillatoria) are autotrophic microbes widely distributed in aquatic and terrestrial environments. Many of them can fix atmospheric nitrogen, serving as crucial biofertilizers, especially in paddy (rice) fields.

Question: The binding energy per nucleon for $_3^7\text{Li}$ and $_2^4\text{He}$ are 5.60 MeV and 7.06 MeV, respectively. In the reaction $_3^7\text{Li} + _1^1\text{H} \rightarrow 2(_2^4\text{He})$, the energy of the proton required to trigger the reaction (assuming products are at rest) is:
Solution:
$BE(\text{reactants}) = 7 \times 5.60 + 0 = 39.2 \text{ MeV}$.

$BE(\text{products}) = 2 \times (4 \times 7.06) = 56.48 \text{ MeV}$.

Energy released $Q = 56.48 - 39.2 = 17.28 \text{ MeV}$.

Question: The bacteria forming blooms in polluted water bodies are:
Solution: The cyanobacteria (also referred to as blue-green algae) have chlorophyll a similar to green plants and are photosynthetic autotrophs. The cyanobacteria are unicellular, colonial or filamentous, freshwater/marine or terrestrial algae. The colonies are generally surrounded by gelatinous sheath. They often form blooms in polluted water bodies.

Question: For a gas sample with ${{\text{N}}_0}$ number of molecules, function ${\text{N}}\left( {\text{V}} \right)$ is given by: ${\text{N}}\left( {\text{V}} \right) = \frac{{{\text{dN}}}}{{{\text{dV}}}} = \left( {\frac{{3{\text{ }}{{\text{N}}_0}}}{{{\text{ V}}_0^3}}} \right){{\text{V}}^2}$ for $0 < {\text{V}} < {{\text{V}}_0}$ and ${\text{N}}\left( {\text{V}} \right) = 0$ for ${\text{V}} > {{\text{V}}_0}$. Where dN is number of molecules in speed range V to ${\text{V}} + {\text{dV}}{\text{.}}$ The rms speed of the molecules is:
Solution:
$V_{rms}^2 = \left\langle {{V^2}} \right\rangle $
$ = \frac{{V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \ldots .}}{{{N_0}}} = \frac{{\smallint {V^2}dN}}{{\smallint dN}}$
here $\frac{{dN}}{{dV}} = N\left( V \right)$

\[V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{{{N_0}}}\int_0^{{V_0}} {N(V){V^2}} dV = \]

\[\frac{1}{{{N_0}}}\int_0^{{V_0}} {\left( {\frac{{3{N_0}}}{{V_0^3}}{V^2}} \right){V^2}} dV\]

\[ = \frac{3}{5}V_0^2 \Rightarrow {V_{rms}} = \sqrt {\frac{3}{5}} {V_0}\]

Question:
Which of the following sequence of T and F is correct for the given statements regarding Group 13 and Group 14 elements?

I. In Group 13, the stability of the +1 oxidation state increases down the group.

lI. $T l^{3+}$ is a strong oxidizing agent.

III. $\mathrm{PbCl}_4$ is more stable than $\mathrm{PbCl}_2$.

IV. $\mathrm{BCl}_3$ acts as a Lewis Acid due to an incomplete octet.
Solution:
Statement I: (True) Due to the Inert Pair Effect, as we move down the group, the $n{{s}^{2}}$ electrons become more reluctant to participate in bonding. Therefore, the lower oxidation state ( +1 for Group 13) becomes increasingly stable.
Statement II: (True) Since $T{{l}^{+}}$is much more stable than $T{{l}^{3+}},T{{l}^{3+}}$ has a high tendency to gain two electrons (get reduced) to reach the +1 state. Hence, it acts as a strong oxidizing agent.
Statement III: (False) For Lead ( Pb ), the +2 oxidation state is much more stable than the +4 state due to the inert pair effect. Therefore, $\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ is more stable than $\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}$. In fact, $\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}$ is thermally unstable and decomposes into $\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$ and $\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$.
Statement IV: (True) Boron in $BC{{l}_{3}}$ has only six electrons in its valence shell. Being electrondeficient, it readily accepts an electron pair from a donor (like $N{{H}_{3}}$ ), thus acting as a Lewis Acid.

Question: Let ${\gamma _1}$ be the ratio of molar specific heat at constant pressure and molar specific heat at constant volume of a monoatomic gas and ${\gamma _2}$ be the similar ratio of diatomic gas. Considering the diatomic gas molecule as a rigid rotator, the ratio, $\frac{{{\gamma _1}}}{{{\gamma _2}}}$ is
Solution:

Question:
Read the following statements concerning the Kinetic Theory of Gases and choose the correct option.
A. The most probable speed of gas molecules is inversely proportional to the square root of the molar mass at a given temperature.
B. At constant temperature and volume, the mean free path of gas molecules is directly proportional to the pressure of the gas.
C. For an ideal monatomic gas at room temperature, the ratio of specific heats $\left( {{C}_{p}}/{{C}_{v}} \right)$ is approximately 1.67.
D. The average velocity vector of all gas molecules in a stationary container in thermal equilibrium is zero.
Solution: Statement A is true $\left( {{v}_{mp}}=\sqrt{\frac{2RT}{M}} \right)$. Statement B is false; mean free path is given by $\lambda =\frac{kT}{\sqrt{2}\pi {{d}^{2}}P}$, meaning it is inversely proportional to pressure. Statement C is true (for monatomic gases, ${{C}_{p}}=\frac{5R}{2}$ and ${{C}_{v}}=\frac{3R}{2}$, so $\gamma =\frac{5}{3}\approx 1.67$ ). Statement D is true; due to random isotropic motion, the vector sum of all molecular velocities is zero.

Question: A nucleus with mass number 240 and binding energy per nucleon as 7.4 MeV breaks into two fragments, each with mass number 120. If each fragment nucleus has binding energy per nucleon as 8.4 MeV, the total energy released in the process is
Solution:
Initial binding energy $ = 240 \times 7.4\,\,{\text{MeV}}$
Final binding energy $ = 2 \times 120 \times 8.4$MeV
Gain in binding energy $ = 240(8.4 - 7.4) = 240\,\,{\text{MeV}}$

Question: The product formed during Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction is
Solution:
Carboxylic acids having an α-hydrogen are halogenated at the a-position on treatment with chlorine or bromine in the presence of small amount of red phosphorus to give a-halo carboxylic acids.

The reaction is known as Hell-Vohard-Zelinsky reaction.

Question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R)
Assertion A: Most enzymes are highly sensitive to changes in their environment, functioning optimally only within a narrow range of temperature and pH
Reason R: Every enzyme exhibits its highest catalytic activity at a specific optimum temperature and optimum pH, which is universally identical for all types of enzymes across different organisms
Options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
Solution: The assertion is true as enzymes generally function within a narrow range of temperature and pH. However, the reason is false. While each enzyme shows its highest activity at a particular optimum temperature and pH, these optimum values are not identical for all enzymes. For example, pepsin functions optimally at an acidic pH (around 1.8), whereas salivary amylase functions optimally at a slightly acidic to neutral pH (around 6.8).

Question: The fundamental biological principle underlying plant tissue culture, defined as the inherent capacity of any living somatic plant cell to divide and generate a complete, mature organism, is known as:
Solution: Cellular totipotency is the unique ability of a single mature or differentiating plant cell to undergo rapid division and differentiation to regenerate an entire, fully functional plant when provided with a sterile, nutrient-rich culture medium.

Question: A uniform metallic rod of length $L$ and square cross-section of side $a$ is made of a material with Young's modulus $Y$. The rod is cooled by a temperature $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }T$. To maintain its original length perfectly, a longitudinal tensile force $F$ is applied to its ends. The coefficient of superficial expansion $\beta $ of the material is (nearly) given by:
Solution:
Cooling the rod causes a natural thermal contraction of magnitude $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{L}_{\text{thermal }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=L\alpha \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }T$, where $\alpha $ is the coefficient of linear expansion.
The applied tensile force $F$ produces a mechanical elongation $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }{{L}_{\text{mech }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{FL}{AY}=\frac{FL}{{{a}^{2}}Y}$. Since the length remains unchanged, the mechanical elongation must exactly offset the thermal contraction: $L\alpha \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }T=\frac{FL}{{{a}^{2}}Y}\Rightarrow \alpha =\frac{F}{{{a}^{2}}Y\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }T}$. The coefficient of superficial (area) expansion is $\beta \approx 2\alpha $, which yields $\beta =\frac{2F}{{{a}^{2}}Y\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }T}$.

Question: The temperature of a hypothetical gas increases to \[\sqrt 2 \] times when compressed adiabatically to half the volume. Its equation can be written as
Solution:
\[{T_1} = T\], \[{T_2} = \sqrt 2 T\]
\[{V_1} = V\], \[{V_2} = \frac{V}{2}\]
For adiabatic process
\[{T_1}V_1^{\gamma - 1} = {T_2}V_2^{\gamma - 1}\]
\[T \times V_1^{\gamma - 1} = \sqrt 2 T \times {\left( {\frac{V}{2}} \right)^{\gamma - 1}}\]
\[{2^{\gamma - 1}} = {2^{\frac{1}{2}}}\]
\[\gamma = \frac{3}{2}\]
\[P{V^{\frac{3}{2}}} = cons\tan t\]

Question: The mass defect for the nucleus of Helium is $0.0303\,\text{u}$. What is the binding energy per nucleon (approximately) for Helium in MeV?
Solution:
Total B.E. $= \Delta m \times 931.5 = 0.0303 \times 931.5 \approx 28.2\,\text{MeV}$.
B.E. per nucleon $= \frac{28.2}{4} \approx 7.05\,\text{MeV}$.
Binding energy per nucleon is the total binding energy divided by the mass number $A$.

Question: The correct order of decreasing acidity of the following aliphatic acids is:
Solution:
\[{\text{FC}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{COOH}} > {\text{ClC}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{COOH}} > {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{COOH}} > {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{COOH}}\]
Acidity is governed by the inductive effect. The electron-withdrawing effect \[\left( { - I} \right)\]increases acidity, and \[F > Cl.\] The electron-donating effect \[\left( { + I} \right)\] decreases acidity, and \[C{H_3}C{H_2}\] has a greater \[ + I\] effect than \[C{H_3}.\]

Question: The root-knot nematode Meloidogyne incognita infects which specific part of the tobacco plant, leading to a severe reduction in crop yield?

Question:

Reason (R): At Curie point a ferromagnetic substance starts behaving as a paramagnetic substance

<!-StartFragment->If Assertion is true but Reason is false.<!-EndFragment->
<!-StartFragment->If Assertion is false but Reason is true.<!-EndFragment->
Solution: The susceptibility of ferromagnetic substance decreases with the rise of temperature in a complicated manner. After Curies point the susceptibility of ferromagnetic substances varies inversely with its absolute temperature. Ferromagnetic substance obeys Curie's law only above its curies point.

Question: An ice cube of dimensions 60 cm × 50 cm × 20 cm is placed in an insulation box of wall thickness 1 cm. The box keeping the ice cube at 0° C of temperature is brought to a room of temperature 40° C The rate of melting of ice is approximately: (Latent heat of fusion of ice is 3.4 × 10<sup>5</sup> J kg<sup>–1</sup> and thermal conducting of insulation wall is 0.05 Wm<sup>–10</sup> C<sup>–1</sup>)
Solution:
\[\frac{{dQ}}{{dt}} = \frac{{KA\Delta T}}{\ell }\]

\[A = 2\left( {0.6 \times 0.5 + 0.5 \times 0.2 + 0.2 \times 0.6} \right)\]

\[ = 2\left( {0.3 + 0.1 + 0.12} \right) = 2\left( {0.4 + 0.12} \right)\] \[ = 2\left( {0.52} \right) = 1.04\;{{\text{m}}^{\text{2}}}\]

\[{R_{th}} = \frac{\ell }{{KA}} \Rightarrow \frac{{1 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{0.05 \times 1.04}} \Rightarrow \frac{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{0.052}}\]

\[\frac{{dd}}{{dt}} = \frac{{\Delta t}}{{{R_{th}}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{40 \times 0.052}}{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}} \Rightarrow 2.08 \times {10^2}\;{\text{J/s}}\]

\[2.08 \times {10^2} = m \times 3.4 \times {10^5}\]

\[m = \frac{{2.08}}{{3.4 \times {{10}^3}}} \Rightarrow 0.61 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{kg/s}}\]

= 61 × 10–5 Kg/s

Question: ${R_1}$ and ${R_2}$ are radii of atomic nuclei of mass number 64 and 27 respectively. The ratio $\left( {{R_1}/{R_2}} \right)$ is:-
Solution: $\frac{{{R_1}}}{{{R_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{A_1}}}{{\;{A_2}}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}} = {\left( {\frac{{64}}{{27}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}} = \frac{4}{3}$

Question: Consider an ideal gas confined in an isolated, thermally insulated closed container. As the gas undergoes a quasi-static adiabatic expansion, the collision frequency between its molecules varies as ${{V}^{q}}$, where $V$ is the volume of the gas. The value of $q$ is given by ( $\gamma ={{C}_{p}}/{{C}_{v}}$ ):
Solution: The collision frequency $\nu $ is proportional to $n{{v}_{\text{rms }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$, where $n=\frac{N}{V}\propto {{V}^{-1}}$ is the molecular number density and ${{v}_{\text{rms }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}\propto \sqrt{T}$. In a reversible adiabatic process, the temperature-volume relationship is given by $T{{V}^{\gamma -1}}=$ constant $\Rightarrow T\propto {{V}^{-\left( \gamma -1 \right)}}$, which implies ${{v}_{\text{rms }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}\propto {{V}^{-\frac{\gamma -1}{2}}}$. Therefore, the collision frequency scales as $\nu \propto {{V}^{-1}}\cdot {{V}^{-\frac{\gamma -1}{2}}}={{V}^{-\frac{\gamma +1}{2}}}$, meaning $q=-\frac{\gamma +1}{2}$.

Question: Light of wavelength 𝜆 shines on a metal surface with intensity X, and the metal emits Y electrons per second of average energy Z. What will happen to Y and Z if  X is doubled?
Options: Y will be doubled and Z will become half.
Y will remain same and Z will be doubled.
Both Y and Z will be doubled.
Y will be double but Z will remain same.
Solution: When intensity is doubled, the number of electrons emitted per second is also doubled, but the average energy of photoelectrons emitted remains the same.

Question: A rod (Young's modulus \[ = 7 \times {10^9}\;{\text{N}}/{{\text{m}}^2}\] has a breaking strain of 0.2 %. The minimum cross sectional area of the rod in order to support a load of 10<sup>4</sup> newtons is:
Solution: \[A = \frac{{FL}}{{Y\Delta L}}\]= 7.1 ×10<sup>–4</sup> m<sup>2</sup>

Question: A rigid vessel contains 2 moles of nitrogen gas ( ${{\text{N}}_{2}}$ ) at an absolute temperature $T$. The pressure exerted by the gas is $P$. An identical vessel contains 2 moles of argon gas (Ar) at an absolute temperature $3T$. Assuming both behave as ideal gases, the pressure of the argon gas is:
Solution: According to the ideal gas law, $PV=nRT$. Since the vessels are identical, the volume $V$ is constant. The number of moles $n=2$ is also the same for both gases. Therefore, the pressure is directly proportional to the absolute temperature ( $P\propto T$ ). Because the temperature of the argon gas ( $3T$ ) is three times that of the nitrogen gas ( $T$ ), its pressure will be $3P$.

Question: If proteins are used as respiratory substrates, they are first degraded by proteases into individual amino acids. Before entering the Krebs cycle, these amino acids may be deaminated and converted directly into which of the following?
Solution: The respiratory pathway is amphibolic. When proteins are used, they break down into amino acids. Depending on their structure, these amino acids undergo deamination and enter the pathway at various stages, primarily as pyruvate, acetyl CoA, or other intermediate $\alpha$-keto acids of the Krebs cycle.

Question: A rubber rope of length 8 m is hung from the ceiling of a room. What is the increase in length of the rope due to its own weight? (Given Young's modulus of elasticity of rubber a 5 × 106 Nm-2 and density of rubber = 1.5 × 103 kgm-3. Take g = 10 ms-2)
Solution:
As, \[Y = \frac{{Mg}}{A} \times \frac{{L/2}}{{\Delta L}}\]

(Length is taken as \[\frac{L}{2}\] because weight acts at centre of gravity (CG) )

Now, \[M = AL\rho \]

(For the purpose of calculation of mass, the whole of geometrical length L is to be considered.)

\[\therefore Y = \frac{{AL\rho gL}}{{2A\Delta L}}\]

or \[\Delta L = \frac{{\rho g{L^2}}}{{2Y}} = \frac{{1.5 \times {{10}^3} \times 10 \times 8 \times 8}}{{2 \times 5 \times {{10}^6}}}\]

\[ = 9.6 \times {10^{ - 2}}m = 9.6 \times {10^{ - 2}} \times {10^3}mm\]

\[ = 96mm\]

Question: Cinnamic acid is β-phenyl-α, β-unsaturated acid. This acid can be prepared from Perkin reaction from:
Solution: The Perkin reaction is used to synthesize $\alpha ,\beta $-unsaturated aromatic acids. It specifically requires an aromatic aldehyde and an acid anhydride in the presence of the sodium or potassium salt of the corresponding acid.

Question: A square lead slab of side 50 cm and thickness 5.0 cm is subjected to a shearing force (on its narrow face) of magnitude 9.0 × 10<sup>4</sup> N. The lower edge is riveted to the floor. The upper edge displacement if the shear modulus of lead is 5.6 × 10<sup>9</sup> Pa will be
Solution:
The lead slab is fixed and force is applied parallel to the narrow face as shown in the figure. Area of the face parallel to which this force is $A = 50{\text{cm}} \times 5{\text{cm}} = 0.5{\text{m}} \times 0.05{\text{m}}$
$ = 25 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{}}{{\text{m}}^{\text{e}}}$
If ${{\Delta L}}$ is the displacement of the upper edge of the slab due to tangential force F , then
$\eta = \frac{{\frac{F}{A}}}{{\frac{{{{\Delta }}L}}{L}}}{{or\Delta }}L = \frac{{FL}}{{\eta A}}$
Substituting the given values, we get
$\begin{gathered}
  {{\Delta L}} = \frac{{\left( {9 \times {{10}^4}{\text{N}}} \right)\left( {0.1} \right)}}{{5.6 \times {{10}^9}{\text{N}}{{\text{m}}^{ - 2}} \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}{m^2}}} \\
  {\text{}} = 3.2 \times {10^{ - 4}}{\text{m}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A spherical black body of radius $R$ at a temperature of ${{27}^{\circ }}\text{C}$ radiates power $P$. If its radius is doubled and its temperature is increased to ${{327}^{\circ }}\text{C}$, what will be the new radiated power?
Solution: According to the Stefan-Boltzmann Law, the power radiated by a black body is $P=\sigma A{{T}^{4}}=\sigma \left( 4\pi {{R}^{2}} \right){{T}^{4}}$. Thus, $P\propto {{R}^{2}}{{T}^{4}}$. The initial temperature is ${{T}_{1}}={{27}^{\circ }}\text{C}=300\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ K}$. The final temperature is ${{T}_{2}}={{327}^{\circ }}\text{C}=600\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ K}$. The new power ${{P}_{2}}$ is evaluated as: $\frac{{{P}_{2}}}{{{P}_{1}}}={{\left( \frac{{{R}_{2}}}{{{R}_{1}}} \right)}^{2}}{{\left( \frac{{{T}_{2}}}{{{T}_{1}}} \right)}^{4}}={{(2)}^{2}}{{\left( \frac{600}{300} \right)}^{4}}=4\times 16=64$. Therefore, ${{P}_{2}}=64P$.

$$\therefore M = 0.4J\,{T^{ - 1}}$$

Question:
In a nuclear fission reactor, a uranium- 235 nucleus captures a thermal neutron and fissions into barium-141, an unknown nucleus $X$, and three neutrons. The reaction is represented as:
$\;_{92}^{235}{\text{U}} + \;_0^1{\text{n}} \to \;_{56}^{141}{\text{Ba}} + X + 3\;_0^1{\text{n}}$
The unknown nucleus $X$ is
Solution: By the principle of conservation of mass number $A$: $235 + 1 = 141 + {A_X} + 3 \Rightarrow 236 = 144 + {A_X} \Rightarrow {A_X} = 92$. By conservation of atomic number $Z:92 + 0 = 56 + {Z_X} + 0 \Rightarrow {Z_X} = 36$. The nucleus with $Z = 36$ and $A = 92$ is $\;_{36}^{92}{\text{Kr}}$.

Question:
Match List I with List II.
List IList II
A.Modulus of RigidityI.$\frac{{F/A}}{{{{\Delta }}L/L}}$
B.Bulk ModulusII.$\frac{1}{{{\text{Bulk Modulus}}}}$
C.CompressibilityIII.$\frac{{F/A}}{\theta }$
D.Young's ModulusIV.$ - V\frac{{{{\Delta }}P}}{{{{\Delta }}V}}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Solution:
Modulus of Rigidity is the ratio of shear stress to shear strain, matching III.
Bulk Modulus is the ratio of volumetric stress to volumetric strain, matching IV. Compressibility is the reciprocal of Bulk Modulus, matching II.
Young's Modulus is the ratio of longitudinal stress to longitudinal strain, matching I.

Question: Select R-isomers from the following
Solution:
Using the Cahn-Ingold-Prelog (CIP) priority rules:
Structure I (Glyceraldehyde): Priority: $-\text{OH}\left( 1 \right)>-\text{CHO}\left( 2 \right)>-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{OH}\left( 3 \right)>$ -H (4). The path $1\to 2\to 3$ is clockwise. Since -H is on the horizontal bond, the configuration is reversed from $S$ to $R$.
Structure II (2-chlorobutane): Priority: $-\text{Cl}\left( 1 \right)>-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\left( 2 \right)>-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\left( 3 \right)>$ -H (4). The path $1\to 2\to 3$ is counter-clockwise. Since -H is horizontal, it is reversed to R .
Structure III (Alanine): Priority: $-\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\left( 1 \right)>-\text{COOH}\left( 2 \right)>-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\left( 3 \right)>-\text{H}\left( 4 \right)$. Path $1\to 2\to 3$ is counter-clockwise. Since -H is vertical, the configuration remains S .

Question: The masses of neutron and proton are 1.0087 a.m.u. and 1.0073 a.m.u. respectively. If the neutrons and protons combine to form a helium nucleus (alpha particle) of mass 4.0015 a.m.u. The binding energy of the helium nucleus will be (1 a.m.u. $ = 931{\text{MeV)}}$
Solution:
\[BE = \left[ {Z{m_p} + \left( {A - Z} \right){m_n} = {M_\alpha }} \right]\]
for a particle ie. Helium Nucleus \[\left( {_3^4He} \right)\]
\[BE = \left[ {2 \times 1.0073 + \left( {4 - 2} \right) \times 1.0087 - 4.0015} \right]931\]
\[ = \left[ {0.0305a.m.u} \right]931 = 28.4Mev\]

Question:
For the vectors $\vec U = \hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k$ and $\vec V = 3\hat i - 4\hat j$, match the expressions in Column I with their corresponding values in Column II.
Column IColumn II
(I)$\left| {\vec U} \right|$$\left( {\text{P}} \right)$$4\hat i - 2\hat j - 2\hat k$
(II)$\left| {\vec V} \right|$$\left( {\text{Q}} \right)$3
(III)$\vec U + \vec V$$\left( {\text{R}} \right)$ $ - 2\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k$
(IV)$\vec U - \vec V$$\left( {{\text{S}}} \right)$5
Options: I → Q, II → S, III → P, IV → R
Solution:
Evaluating each expression in Column I:
Magnitude of $\vec U:\left| {\vec U} \right| = \sqrt {{{(1)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{( - 2)}^2}} = \sqrt {1 + 4 + 4} = \sqrt 9 = 3$. This matches (I) → (Q).
Magnitude of $\vec V:\left| {\vec V} \right| = \sqrt {{{(3)}^2} + {{( - 4)}^2} + {{(0)}^2}} = \sqrt {9 + 16 + 0} = \sqrt {25} = 5$. This matches (II) → (S).
Vector addition: $\vec U + \vec V = \left( {1 + 3} \right)\hat i + \left( {2 - 4} \right)\hat j + \left( { - 2 + 0} \right)\hat k = 4\hat i - 2\hat j - 2\hat k$. This matches (III) → (P).
Vector subtraction: $\vec U - \vec V = \left( {1 - 3} \right)\hat i + \left( {2 - \left( { - 4} \right)} \right)\hat j + \left( { - 2 - 0} \right)\hat k = - 2\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k$. This matches (IV) → (R).
Therefore, the correct mapping is ${\text{I}} \to {\text{Q}}$, II → S , III $ \to {\text{P}}$, IV $ \to {\text{R}}$.

Question: The ratio of magnetic field at two points P and Q on the axis of a short bar magnet, which are respectively at distance 2.4 cm and 4.8 cm from its centre is
Solution:
$${B_{{\text{axial }}}} = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }} \cdot \frac{{2M}}{{{r^3}}} \Rightarrow {B_{{\text{axial }}}} \propto \frac{1}{{{r^3}}}$$

Question:
Regarding the periodic properties of Group 13 elements, which of the following statements is correct?
(A) Gallium has a smaller atomic radius than Aluminum due to poor shielding by d-electrons.
(B) The first ionization enthalpy of Thallium is higher than that of Indium.
(C) Electronegativity increases from Aluminum to Thallium.
Solution: All statements are correct. Gallium's radius is smaller due to the d-block contraction. Thallium's higher IE and electronegativity compared to Indium are due to poor shielding by 4 f and 5 d electrons (effective nuclear charge increase).

Question: When 200 g of a liquid A at ${{80}^{\circ }}\text{C}$ is mixed with 100 g of a liquid B at ${{40}^{\circ }}\text{C}$ in a calorimeter of negligible heat capacity, the equilibrium temperature of the mixture becomes ${{60}^{\circ }}\text{C}$. If 100 g of liquid A at ${{90}^{\circ }}\text{C}$ is added to 100 g of liquid B at ${{30}^{\circ }}\text{C}$, the final equilibrium temperature of the mixture will be:
Solution: From the principle of calorimetry for the first scenario: Heat lost $=$ Heat gained $\Rightarrow {{m}_{A}}{{s}_{A}}\left( {{T}_{A}}-{{T}_{m}} \right)={{m}_{B}}{{s}_{B}}\left( {{T}_{m}}-{{T}_{B}} \right)\Rightarrow 200\cdot {{s}_{A}}$. $\left( 80-60 \right)=100\cdot {{s}_{B}}\cdot \left( 60-40 \right)\Rightarrow 4000{{s}_{A}}=2000{{s}_{B}}\Rightarrow {{s}_{B}}=2{{s}_{A}}$. For the second scenario, let $T$ be the final mixture temperature: $100\cdot {{s}_{A}}\cdot \left( 90-T \right)=100\cdot {{s}_{B}}\cdot \left( T-30 \right)$$\Rightarrow {{s}_{A}}\left( 90-T \right)=2{{s}_{A}}\left( T-30 \right)$$\Rightarrow 90-T=2T-60\Rightarrow 3T=150\Rightarrow T={{50}^{\circ }}\text{C}$.

Question:
Assertion (A): In Group 13, the atomic radius of gallium is smaller than that of aluminium.
Reason (R): The atomic radius of gallium is smaller than that of aluminium because p-electrons of gallium provide better shielding effect.
Choose the correct option:
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Assertion is false, but Reason is true.
Solution:
Assertion is true: Gallium has a slightly smaller atomic radius than aluminium due to poor shielding by intervening 3d electrons.
Reason is false: The decrease in radius is not due to better shielding by p-electrons. It is caused by the poor shielding effect of d-electrons, which increases effective nuclear charge.
Therefore, Assertion is true, but Reason is false.

Question: Young's modulus of brass and steel are 10 × 1010 N/m2 and 2 × 1011 N/m2, respectively. A brass wire and a steel wire of the same length are extended by 1 mm under the same force. The radii of the brass and steel wires are RB and RS respectively. Then

Question:
Three copper blocks of masses ${{\text{M}}_1},{{\text{M}}_2}$ and ${{\text{M}}_3}{\text{ kg}}$, respectively are brought into thermal contact till they reach equilibrium. Before contact, they were at temperatures ${{\text{T}}_1}$, ${{\text{T}}_2},{\text{}}{{\text{T}}_3}\left( {{\text{}}{{\text{T}}_1} > {{\text{T}}_2} > {{\text{T}}_3}} \right)$. Assuming there is no heat loss to the surroundings, the equilibrium temperature T is:
(s is specific heat of copper)
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  (\Delta Q)\,system = 0 \\
  \Delta {Q_1} + \Delta {Q_2} + \Delta {Q_3} = 0 \\
  {{\text{M}}_1}\;{\text{S}}\left( {\;{\text{T}} - {{\text{T}}_1}} \right) + {{\text{M}}_2}\;{\text{S}}\left( {\;{\text{T}} - {{\text{T}}_2}} \right) + {{\text{M}}_3}\;{\text{S}}\left( {\;{\text{T}} - {{\text{T}}_3}} \right) = 0 \\
\end{gathered} $
${{\text{M}}_1}\;{\text{T}} - {{\text{M}}_1}\;{{\text{T}}_1} + {{\text{M}}_2}\;{\text{T}} - {{\text{M}}_2}\;{{\text{T}}_2} + {{\text{M}}_3}\;{\text{T}} - {{\text{M}}_3}\;{{\text{T}}_3} = 0$
${\text{T}} = \frac{{{{\text{M}}_1}\;{{\text{T}}_1} + {{\text{M}}_2}\;{{\text{T}}_2} + {{\text{M}}_3}\;{{\text{T}}_3}}}{{{{\text{M}}_1} + {{\text{M}}_2} + {{\text{M}}_3}}}$

Question: A uniform cylindrical wire of initial length $L$ and radius $r$ is subjected to a longitudinal stretching force within its elastic limit. If the Poisson's ratio of the material is $\sigma $, the fractional change in its volume ($\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }V}{V}$) in terms of its longitudinal strain ($\epsilon =\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }L}{L}$) is mathematically expressed as:
Solution: The geometric volume of a cylindrical wire is $V=\pi {{r}^{2}}L$. Applying logarithmic differentiation yields the fractional change in volume: $\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }V}{V}=2\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }r}{r}+\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }L}{L}$. Poisson's ratio is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain: $\sigma =-\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }r/r}{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }L/L}$, which rearranges to $\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }r}{r}=-\sigma \epsilon $. Substituting this relation back into the volume equation provides $\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }V}{V}=2\left( -\sigma \epsilon  \right)+\epsilon =\left( 1-2\sigma  \right)\epsilon $.

Question:
The correct statements from the following are:
(A) Tl+ is a powerful oxidising agent
(B) Al3+ does not get reduced easily
(C) Both Al3+ and Tl3+ are very stable in solution
(D) ${\text{T}}{{\text{l}}^ + }$ is more stable than Tl3+
(E) Al3+ and Tl+ are highly stable
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Solution:
(i) False, ${\text{T}}{{\text{l}}^{ + }}$ is not an oxidizing agent
(ii) True, $E{^\circ _{A{l^{3 + }}/Al}} = 1.66V$. So, it is difficult to reduce ${\text{A}}{{\text{l}}^{3 + }}$.
So ${\text{A}}{{\text{l}}^{3 + }}$ is highly stable.
(iii) False, while ${\text{A}}{{\text{l}}^{3 + }}$ is stable, ${\text{T}}{{\text{l}}^{3 + }}$ is unstable and is reduced easily to ${\text{T}}{{\text{l}}^ + }$.
(iv) True, ${\text{T}}{{\text{l}}^ + }$ is more stable than ${\text{T}}{{\text{l}}^{3 + }}$.
(v) True, both ${\text{A}}{{\text{l}}^{3 + }}$ and ${\text{T}}{{\text{l}}^ + }$ are stable in solution due to their high charge density.

Question: If $\vec a = 2i + 6j + m\hat k$ and $\vec b = n\hat i + 12j + 4\hat k$ are parallel to each other, then product of m and n is:-

For vectors to be parallel:
$\frac{{{a_x}}}{{{b_x}}} = \frac{{{a_y}}}{{{b_y}}} = \frac{{{a_z}}}{{{b_z}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{2}{n} = \frac{6}{{12}} = \frac{m}{4}$
\[ \Rightarrow m = 2\] and \[n = 4\]
\[ \Rightarrow mn = 2\left( 4 \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow mn = 8\]

2 boron atoms and 4 terminal hydrogen atoms lie in the same plane and 2 bridging hydrogen atoms lie in the perpendicular plane

As seen from the structure drawn, 6 electrons are required for the formation of conventional covalent bond structure by B-atom, whereas in diborane, there are 12 valence electrons, three from each boron atoms and six from the six hydrogen atoms. Question: A light steel wire of length 2 m has a Young's modulus of $2 \times {10^{11}}{\text{N}}/{{\text{m}}^2}$. It is hung from a ceiling and a mass m is hung at the other end. If the maximum stress it can sustain is $4 \times {10^8}{\text{N}}/{{\text{m}}^2}$, the maximum increase in length is:
Solution:
Young's Modulus $Y = \frac{{{\text{Stress}}}}{{{\text{Strain}}}} = \frac{{{\text{Stress}}}}{{{{\Delta }}L/L}}$
${{\Delta }}L = \frac{{{\text{Stress}} \times L}}{Y} = \frac{{\left( {4 \times {{10}^8}} \right) \times 2}}{{2 \times {{10}^{11}}}}$
${{\Delta }}L = 4 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{m}} = 4{\text{mm}}$

Question:

B.Bulk ModulusII.Ratio of longitudinal stress to longitudinal strain
C.Shear ModulusIII.Ratio of hydraulic stress to volume strain
D.Poisson's RatioIV.Ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Solution: Young's Modulus relates to longitudinal changes. Bulk Modulus relates to volume changes under pressure. Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity) relates to shape change. Poisson's ratio is the ratio of lateral to longitudinal strain.

Question:
Assertion (A): Thallium (Tl) shows +3 oxidation state compounds more stable than its +1 oxidation state compounds.
Reason (R): Due to the inert pair effect, the valence ns² electrons of heavier p-block elements tend to remain non-bonding.
Options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is false, but Reason is true.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Solution:
Thallium belongs to Group 13 with valence configuration: 6s² 6p¹
Because of the inert pair effect, the 6s² electrons do not participate easily in bonding.
Therefore:
+1 oxidation state (loss of only 6p¹ electron) is more stable
+3 oxidation state is less stable
So the Assertion is false, but the Reason is true.
Correct Option: (c)

Question: Which of the following correctly describes the role of the hepatic portal system?
Options: It transports oxygenated blood from the liver to the intestines
It carries nutrient-rich blood from the intestines to the liver for processing
It directly connects the heart with the kidneys for blood filtration
It supplies deoxygenated blood to the brain
Solution: The hepatic portal vein transports nutrient-rich blood from the small intestine to the liver, where it is processed and detoxified before entering general circulation.

Question:
Identify the correct statement for ${{\text{B}}_2}{{\text{H}}_6}$ from those given below:
(A) In ${{\text{B}}_2}{{\text{H}}_6}$, all B-H bonds are equivalent.
(B) In ${{\text{B}}_2}{{\text{H}}_6}$, there are four 3-centre 2-electron bonds
(C) ${{\text{B}}_2}{{\text{H}}_6}$, is a Lewis acid
(D) In ${{\text{B}}_2}{{\text{H}}_6}$, there are two 3-centre 2-electron bonds
(E) ${{\text{B}}_2}{{\text{H}}_6}$, is planar molecule
Solution:
${{\text{B}}_2}{{\text{H}}_6}$ has two 3-centre-2-electron bonds. In ${{\text{B}}_2}{{\text{H}}_6}$, bridge bonds have greater bond length as compared to terminal bonds. Also, bridge bonds lie in one plane while terminal ones lie in perpendicular plane.
It is an deficient species and hence, acts as a Lewis acid.

Question:
Arrange the following steps in the correct sequence for generating a somatic hybrid plant:
A. Fusion of the isolated protoplasts
B. Isolation of single cells from the mesophyll tissue of two different plant varieties
C. Regeneration of a complete hybrid plantlet from the cultured tissue
D. Enzymatic digestion of the cell walls using cellulase and pectinase to release naked protoplasts
E. Culturing the hybrid protoplast on a suitable nutrient medium
Solution: The process of somatic hybridization starts with isolating single cells from the desired plants (B). The cell walls are then digested enzymatically to obtain naked protoplasts (D). Protoplasts from two different varieties are then fused together (A). The resulting hybrid protoplast is cultured on a nutrient medium to form a callus (E), which eventually regenerates into a new, somatic hybrid plant (C).

Question:
Which of the following organisms are capable of fixing atmospheric nitrogen and are widely used as biofertilizers in paddy fields?
(i) Anabaena
(ii) Nostoc
(iii) Rhizobium
(iv) Oscillatoria
Solution: Cyanobacteria such as Anabaena, Nostoc, and Oscillatoria are autotrophic microbes widely distributed in aquatic and terrestrial environments, many of which can fix atmospheric nitrogen. They serve as an important biofertilizer in paddy (rice) fields. Rhizobium is a symbiotic bacterium that fixes nitrogen in association with the roots of leguminous plants, not typically free-living in paddy fields.

Question:
Select the correct statement :
I. Trichoderma species are free-living fungi that are very common in root ecosystems and act as effective biological control agents against several plant pathogens
II. Baculoviruses belonging to the genus Nucleopolyhedrovirus are excellent candidates for species-specific, broad-spectrum insecticidal applications
III. The biologically active chemical drug reserpine, which shows variations in potency, is obtained from the medicinal plant Rauwolfia vomitoria
IV. Micropropagation via tissue culture is heavily utilized to rapidly produce thousands of plants that are genetically identical to the original plant (somaclones)
Options: I, II, and III are correct
II, III, and IV are correct
I, III, and IV are correct
Solution: Baculoviruses of the genus Nucleopolyhedrovirus are excellent candidates for species-specific, narrow-spectrum (not broad-spectrum) insecticidal applications. Because they target only a single species or a limited group of insects, they have no negative impacts on non-target organisms, which makes them ideal for integrated pest management (IPM).

Question: If the coefficient of linear expansion of a metal is ${\mathbf{0}}{\mathbf{.00002}}\,{{\mathbf{K}}^{{\mathbf{ - 1}}}}$, then the necessary increase in temperature of the metal rod in order to increase its length by 2% is
Solution:
Coefficient of linear expansion \[ = 0.00002{K^{ - 1}} = 2 \times {10^{ - 5}}{K^{ - 1}}\]
\[L' = L + \frac{2}{{100}}L = \frac{{102}}{{100}}L\]
Increase in length of metal rod
\[\Delta L = L \alpha \Delta T\] (ΔT is change in temp)
\[\frac{2}{{100}}L = L \times 2 \times {10^{ - 5}}\Delta T\]
\[\frac{1}{{{{10}^2} \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}} = \Delta T\]
\[\frac{1}{{{{10}^{ - 5}}}} = \Delta T\]
\[\frac{1}{{{{10}^{ - 3}}}} = {10^3} = \Delta T\]
\[\Delta T = 1000\,{\text{K}}\]

Question: If the total binding energies of $\;_1^2H,\,\,\;_2^4He,\;\,\,\;_{26}^{56}Fe$ &$\;_{92}^{235}U$ nuclei are 2.22, 28.3, 492 and 1786 MeV respectively, identify the most stable nucleus of the following.

\[_{26}F{e^{56}}:\frac{{492}}{{56}} = 8.78\;Mev\]

Question:
Assertion (A): $T{{l}^{3+}}$ is a much stronger oxidizing agent than $A{{l}^{3+}}$.
Reason (R): The "inert pair effect" becomes more pronounced as we move down Group 13, making the +1 oxidation state more stable than the +3 state for heavier elements.
Options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A .
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.
Solution: Due to the poor shielding of $d$ and $f$ electrons, the $6{{s}^{2}}$ electrons of Thallium are held tightly by the nucleus. This makes $T{{l}^{3+}}$ unstable, prompting it to gain electrons (act as an oxidizing agent) to reach the more stable $T{{l}^{+}}$state.

Question: A thermally insulated vessel contains a non-reacting gas mixture consisting of 2.0 moles of a rigid diatomic gas and 4.0 moles of a rigid non-linear polyatomic gas at an absolute temperature $T$. Assuming all vibrational degrees of freedom are inactive, what is the total internal energy of the gaseous mixture?
Solution: The internal energy of an ideal gas is given by $U = n\frac{f}{2}RT$, where $f$ is the number of active degrees of freedom. For a rigid diatomic gas (e.g., ${N_2}$ ), there are 3 translational and 2 rotational degrees of freedom, so ${f_1} = 5$. For a rigid non-linear polyatomic gas (e.g., $C{H_4}$ ), there are 3 translational and 3 rotational degrees of freedom, so ${f_2} = 6$. Total internal energy ${U_{{\text{total}}}} = {U_1} + {U_2} = {n_1}\frac{{{f_1}}}{2}RT + {n_2}\frac{{{f_2}}}{2}RT$. Substituting the given values: ${U_{{\text{total}}}} = 2.0\left( {\frac{5}{2}} \right)RT + 4.0\left( {\frac{6}{2}} \right)RT = 5RT + 12RT = 17RT$.

Question: Which specific metal ion serves as a crucial biochemical co-factor for the enzyme carbonic anhydrase, as well as for the proteolytic enzyme carboxypeptidase?
Solution: Metal ions form coordination bonds with side chains at the active site and simultaneously form coordination bonds with the substrate. Zinc ($Zn^{2+}$) acts as a highly specific co-factor for both carbonic anhydrase (essential for $CO_2$ transport in blood) and carboxypeptidase (a pancreatic enzyme involved in protein digestion).

Question: In a meter bridge experiment, the null point is obtained at 40 cm from the left end. It is later discovered that the bridge wire has end errors: the left end has a resistance equivalent to 1 cm of wire and the right end has a resistance equivalent to 2 cm. If the resistance in the left gap is $10\text{ }\!\!\Omega\!\!\text{ }$, calculate the true value of the unknown resistance in the right gap.
Solution: Let $l$ be the observed balance point. The corrected lengths are ${{l}_{1}}=l+\alpha $ and ${{l}_{2}}=\left( 100-l \right)+\beta $. Given $l=40,\alpha =1$, and $\beta =2.{{l}_{1}}=40+1=41\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ cm}$ and ${{l}_{2}}=60+2=62\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ cm}$. Using the bridge condition: $\frac{R}{X}=\frac{{{l}_{1}}}{{{l}_{2}}}\Rightarrow \frac{10}{X}=\frac{41}{62}$. Therefore, $X=\frac{620}{41}\approx 15.12\text{ }\!\!\Omega\!\!\text{ }$.

Question: A radioactive isotope $\;_Z^AP$ undergoes a sequence of spontaneous decays to form a stable nucleus $\;_{Z - 2}^{A - 4}S$. The decay sequence is observed as follows: $\;_Z^AP \to \;_{Z + 1}^AQ \to \;_{Z - 1}^{A - 4}R \to \;_{Z - 2}^{A - 4}S$. Which of the following correctly identifies the sequence of emitted particles?
Solution: In the first step, $\;_Z^AP \to \;_{Z + 1}^AQ$, the atomic number increases by 1 while the mass number remains unchanged, indicating the emission of a ${\beta ^ - }$particle (electron). In the second step, $\;_{Z + 1}^AQ \to \;_{Z - 1}^{A - 4}R$, the atomic number decreases by 2 and the mass number decreases by 4 , indicating the emission of an $\alpha $ particle (helium nucleus). In the final step, $\;_{Z - 1}^{A - 4}R \to \;_{Z - 2}^{A - 4}S$, the atomic number decreases by 1 while the mass number remains unchanged, indicating the emission of a ${\beta ^ + }$particle (positron). Thus, the sequence is ${\beta ^ - },\alpha ,{\beta ^ + }$.

Question: During the secondary treatment of sewage, the "Flocs" consist of:
Options: Masses of bacteria associated with fungal filaments
Solution: Flocs are mesh-like structures formed by aerobic bacteria and fungi that consume organic matter in the aeration tank.

Question: The total radiant energy per unit area, normal to the direction of incidence, received at a distance R from the center of a star of radius r, whose outer surface radiates as a black body at a temperature T K is given by (where ${\mathbf{\sigma }}$ is  Stefan's constant)
Solution:
From stefan’s (law)
\[E = \sigma eA{T^4}\] [(e = 1 (black body)]
⇒\[E = \sigma A{T^4}\]
⇒\[E = \sigma \left( {4\pi {r^2}} \right){T^4}\]
\[I = \frac{E}{A} = \frac{{\sigma \left( {4\pi {r^2}} \right){T^4}}}{{4\pi {R^2}}}\]
\[ = \frac{{\sigma {r^2}{T^4}}}{{{R^2}}}\]

Question: A 2 m long light metal rod AB is suspended from the ceiling horizontally by means of two vertical wires of equal length tied to its ends. One wire is of brass and a has cross-section of $0.2 \times {10^{ - 4}}{\text{}}{{\text{m}}^2}$ and the other is of steel with $0.1 \times {10^{ - 4}}{\text{}}{{\text{m}}^2}$ cross-section. in order to have equal stresses in the two wires, a weight is hung from the rod. The position of the weight along the rod from end A should be
Solution:
Brass A1 = 0.2 × 10–4 m2 σ1

Steel A2 = 0.1 × 10–4 m2 σ2

σ1 = σ2

\[ \frac{{{T_1}}}{{{A_1}}} = \frac{{{T_2}}}{{{A_2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(1)\]

T1 + T2 = W …(2)

 \[  \Sigma {\tau _A} = 0 \]

\[W \times x - {T_2} \times (2) = 0 \]

\[2{T_2} = Wx\]

\[{T_2} = \frac{{Wx}}{2}\]in (1)

\[{T_1} + \frac{{Wx}}{2} = W \]

\[ {T_1} = \left( {W - \frac{{Wx}}{2}} \right) = W\left[ {x - \frac{x}{2}} \right] \]

Put T1 and T2 in (1) 
\[ \frac{{W\left[ {1 - \frac{x}{2}} \right]}}{{\frac{{Wx}}{2}}} = 2 \Rightarrow \frac{{2 - x}}{{2 \times \frac{x}{2}}} = 2 \]
\[ \Rightarrow 2 - x = 2 \Rightarrow 2 = 3x \]
\[ x = \frac{2}{3} = 0.66\,m \]
\[ x = 66.6 \]

Question: What is the product formed when phenyl magnesium bromide $\left( {{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{MgBr} \right)$ reacts with dry ice ( $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ ) followed by acidic hydrolysis?
Solution:
Step 1: The Grignard reagent ( ${{\text{C}}_{6}}\text{H}_{5}^{-}$nucleophile) attacks the electrophilic carbon of $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ :
\[{{C}_{6}}{{H}_{5}}MgBr+C{{O}_{2}}\xrightarrow{dry\,\,ether}{{C}_{6}}{{H}_{5}}COOMgBr\]
Step 2: Acidic hydrolysis:
${{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{COOMgBr}+{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}\to {{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{COOH}+\text{Mg}\left( \text{OH} \right)\text{Br}$
Product: Benzoic acid - this is the standard method for converting aryl halides to one-carbon-higher carboxylic acids via the Grignard $+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ route.
Note: benzaldehyde would require reaction with DMF or HCO -R; benzyl alcohol requires HCHO ; acetophenone requires $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CN}$.

Question: A unique feature of an amino acid is its ability to act as an acid or a base depending on the pH. The form of the amino acid that carries both a positive and a negative charge is called the:
Solution: In solutions of different pH, the structure of amino acids changes. The Zwitterionic form contains both $NH_3^+$ and $COO^-$ groups.

Question: In the given reaction sequence $\left( X \right)$ is:
Solution:
In the given reaction sequence, ( $X$ ) is (a) $RCN$.
Nitriles ( $RCN$ ) undergo partial hydrolysis in basic conditions ( ${{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}/{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }$) to form amides ( ${\text{RCON}}{{\text{H}}_2}$).
The resulting amide is then further hydrolyzed in acidic medium $\left( {{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}/{{\text{H}}^ + }} \right)$to yield the final carboxylic acid (RCOOH).

Question:
Assertion (A): During the secondary treatment of sewage, the Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD) of the effluent is significantly reduced
Reason (R): The anaerobic sludge digesters actively consume the major part of the organic matter in the effluent during this specific stage to reduce the BOD
Options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
Solution: The Assertion is true as secondary treatment reduces BOD. However, the Reason is false. It is the vigorous growth of aerobic microbes (flocs), not anaerobic sludge digesters, that consumes the organic matter and reduces the BOD. Anaerobic sludge digesters are used later in the process to digest the bacterial flocs.

Boron forms B³⁺ ions readily like Al³⁺

Question:
The correct orders among the following are:
Atomic radius: $B < {\text{Al}} < {\text{Ga}} < {\text{In}} < {\text{Tl}}$
Electronegativity: ${\text{Al}} < {\text{Ga}} < {\text{In}} < {\text{Tl}} < B$
Density: ${\text{Tl}} < {\text{In}} < {\text{Ga}} < {\text{Al}} < B$
1st Ionisation Energy: ${\text{In}} < {\text{Al}} < {\text{Ga}} < {\text{Tl}} < B$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Solution:
Atomic Radius order:
\[{\text{Tl}} > {\text{In}} > {\text{Al}} > {\text{Ga}} > {\text{B}}\]
Density Order:
\[{\text{Tl}} > {\text{In}} > {\text{Ga}} > {\text{Al}} > {\text{B}}\]
Atomic radius of Ga is less than that of Al due to presence of additional 10 d-orbitals having poor shielding effect.
So, order of atomic radii is:
\[{\text{B}} < {\text{Ga}} < {\text{Al}} < {\text{In}} < {\text{Tl}}\]
and order of ionization energy
so, order is
\[{\text{In}} < {\text{Al}} < {\text{Ga}} < {\text{Tl}} < {\text{B}}\]

Question: The greater BOD of wastewater
Options: Relates the decrease in temperature of water
Solution: The greater BOD of wastewater decreases the oxygen content of water. Biochemical oxygen demand (BOD) is an amount of dissolved oxygen that would be consumed if all the organic matter in one liter of water were oxidized by microorganisms (at 20°C). It is an indicator of water quality. If the value of BOD is high then water is highly polluted by organic matter. If the value of BOD is low then water is either normal or less polluted by organic matter.

Question: A 2 m long rod is suspended with the help of two wires of equal length. One wire is of steel and its cross-sectional area is $0.1{\text{c}}{{\text{m}}^2}$ and another wire is of brass and its cross-sectional area is $0.2{\text{c}}{{\text{m}}^2}$. If a load w is suspended from the rod and stress produced in both the wires is same, then the ratio of tensions in them will be :-
Solution:
${\text{Stress}} = \frac{{{\text{Tension}}}}{{{\text{Area}}}}$
Given that the stress in both wires is the same,
$\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{A_1}}} = \frac{{{T_2}}}{{{A_2}}}$
where
${A_1} = 0.1{\text{c}}{{\text{m}}^2}{\text{}}\left( {{\text{steel}}} \right),{\text{}}{A_2} = 0.2{\text{c}}{{\text{m}}^2}{\text{}}\left( {{\text{brass}}} \right)$
Therefore,
$\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}} = \frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}} = \frac{{0.1}}{{0.2}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$
Hence,
$\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}} = 0.5$

Question: At what temperature will the root mean square (r.m.s.) speed of nitrogen molecules $\left( {{\text{N}}_{2}},M=28\text{u} \right)$ be exactly equal to the r.m.s. speed of hydrogen molecules ( ${{\text{H}}_{2}},M=2\text{u}$ ) at $-{{73}^{\circ }}\text{C}$ ?
Solution: Let ${{T}_{{{\text{H}}_{2}}}}=-{{73}^{\circ }}\text{C}=200\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ K}$. Since ${{v}_{\text{rms}}}=\sqrt{\frac{3RT}{M}}$, equating the speeds gives $\frac{{{T}_{{{\text{N}}_{2}}}}}{{{M}_{{{\text{N}}_{2}}}}}=\frac{{{T}_{{{\text{H}}_{2}}}}}{{{M}_{{{\text{H}}_{2}}}}}$. Substituting the known values: $\frac{{{T}_{{{\text{N}}_{2}}}}}{28}=\frac{200}{2}\Rightarrow {{T}_{{{\text{N}}_{2}}}}=2800\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ K}$. Converting to Celsius gives $2800-273={{2527}^{\circ }}\text{C}$.

Question: A nucleus has a mass of $3.32 \times {10^{ - 26}}{\text{ kg}}$. Given the average nuclear density is $2.3 \times {10^{17}}{\text{ kg}}/{{\text{m}}^3}$, its mass number $A$ is approximately:
Solution: Mass of a nucleus is $M \approx A \times {m_u}$, where ${m_u} \approx 1.66 \times {10^{ - 27}}{\text{ kg}}$ is the atomic mass unit. $A = \frac{{3.32 \times {{10}^{ - 26}}}}{{1.66 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}}} = 20$.

Question: The strain energy stored in a body of volume V due to shear strain \[\phi \] is (shear modulus is $\eta $)
Solution:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}
  {{\text{Shear modulus}} = \frac{{{\text{ Shear stress }}}}{{{\text{ Shear stress }}}}} \\
  {\eta = \frac{{{\text{ Shear stress }}}}{\phi }} \\
  {\eta \phi = {\text{ Shear stress }}}
\end{array}$
Strain energy per unit volume = ½ × shear stress × shear strain
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \frac{{{\text{ Strain energy }}}}{{{\text{ Volume }}}} = \frac{1}{2} \times \eta \phi \times \phi \quad {\text{ (Cross multiply volume) }} \\
  {\text{Strain energy}} = \frac{1}{2}\eta {\phi ^2}V \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: The rubber cord catapult has a cross-sectional area 1 mm<sup>2</sup> and total unstretched length 10.0 cm. It is stretched to 12.0 cm and then released to project a missile of mass 5.0 g. Taking Young's modulus for rubber as 5.0 × 10<sup>8</sup> Nm<sup>–2</sup>, the tension in the cord is
Solution:
A = 1 mm2 = 10–6 m2

Li = 10 cm, Lf = 12 cm

∆L = Lf – Li = 1 cm = 2 × 10–2 m

m = 5g = 5 × 10–3 kg

Y = 5 × 108 N/m2

F = stress × A = Y × strain × A

\[ = Y \times \frac{{\Delta L}}{{{L_i}}} \times A = 5 \times {10^8} \times \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{10 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}} \times {10^{ - 6}}\]

\[ = 5 \times 2 \times {10^8} \times {10^{ - 7}} = 10 \times 10 = 100\,\,{\text{N}}\]

Question: In a vernier calipers, 10 divisions of the vernier scale coincide with 9 divisions of the main scale. If $1{\text{MSD}} = 1{\text{mm}}$, the least count (vernier constant) is:
Solution:
Least Count $\left( {LC} \right) = 1{\text{MSD}} - 1{\text{VSD}}$.
$10{\text{VSD}} = 9{\text{MSD}} \Rightarrow 1{\text{VSD}}$
$ = 0.9{\text{MSD}} = 0.9{\text{mm}}$
$LC = 1{\text{mm}} - 0.9{\text{mm}}$
$ = 0.1{\text{mm}} = 0.01{\text{cm}}$

Question: The magnitude of the magnetic field produced by a short bar magnet at a distance $d$ from its center on its equatorial line is ${{B}_{0}}$. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field produced by the same magnet at a distance of $3d$ from its center on its axial line?
Solution: For a short bar magnet of magnetic moment $M$, the magnetic field on the equatorial line at a distance $d$ is ${{B}_{\text{eq }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{{{\mu }_{0}}}{4\pi }\frac{M}{{{d}^{3}}}={{B}_{0}}$. The magnetic field on the axial line at a distance $r$ is given by ${{B}_{\text{ax}}}=\frac{{{\mu }_{0}}}{4\pi }\frac{2M}{{{r}^{3}}}$. Substituting $r=3d$ into the axial field equation yields ${{B}_{\text{ax}}}=\frac{{{\mu }_{0}}}{4\pi }\frac{2M}{{{(3d)}^{3}}}=\frac{{{\mu }_{0}}}{4\pi }\frac{2M}{27{{d}^{3}}}$. By substituting the initial condition ${{B}_{0}}$, this simplifies directly to ${{B}_{\text{ax}}}=\frac{2}{27}{{B}_{0}}$.

Question:
Match the following enzymes given in column I with the reactions they catalyse given in column II and select the correct option:
Column I (Enzymes)Column II (Reactions)
AInvertaseiDecomposition of urea into NH3 and CO2
BMaltaseiiConversion of glucose into ethyl alcohol
CPepsiniiiHydrolysis of maltose into glucose
DUreaseivHydrolysis of cane sugar
EZymasevHydrolysis of proteins into peptides
Options: A → iv; B → ii; C → v; D → iii; E → i
A → iv; B → iii; C → v; D → i; E → ii
A → iv; B → i; C → iii; D → v; E → ii
A → iv; B → v; C → ii; D → iii; E → i
Solution:
(A) Invertase: Catalyzes the (iv) Hydrolysis of cane sugar (sucrose) into glucose and fructose.
(B) Maltase: Catalyzes the (iii) Hydrolysis of maltose into glucose.
(C) Pepsin: Catalyzes the (v) Hydrolysis of proteins into peptides in the stomach.
(D) Urease: Catalyzes the (i) Decomposition of urea into ${\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}$ and ${\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_2}$.
(E) Zymase: Catalyzes the (ii) Conversion of glucose into ethyl alcohol and carbon dioxide during fermentation.

Question: In a room where the temperature is 30<sup>o</sup>C, the body cools from 61<sup>o</sup>C to 59<sup>o</sup>C in 1 minutes. What time will the body take to cool from 51<sup>o</sup>C to 49<sup>o</sup>C in the same room?
Solution:
\[ \;{T_{mean}} = \;\frac{{(61 + 59)\,^\circ C}}{2} = 60\,^\circ C\]
\[\frac{{\Delta T}}{{\Delta t}} = k\left( {{T_{mean}} - T} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{2}{1} = k\left( {60 - 30} \right)\,\,^\circ C\]
\[k = \frac{2}{{30}} = \frac{1}{{15}}\]
Now, \[{T_1} = 51\,\,^\circ C,\;\,{T_2} = 49\,\,^\circ C\]
\[\Delta T = 2\,\,^\circ C\]
\[\Delta t = ?\]
\[{T_{mean}} = \frac{{(51 + 49)\,\,^\circ C}}{2} = 50\,\,^\circ C\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta T}}{{\Delta t}} = k\left( {{T_{mean}} - T} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{2}{{\Delta t}} = \frac{1}{{15}}\left( {50 - 30} \right)\,\,^\circ C = \frac{{20}}{{15}}\]
\[\Delta t = 1.5\;\min \]

Question: A block of ice at –12° C is slowly heated and converted into steam at 100°C. Which of the following curves best represents the event?

A tuning fork of 512 Hz is used to produce resonance in a resonance tube experiment. The level of water at first resonance is 30.7 cm and at second resonance is 63.2 cm. The error in calculating velocity of sound is:
[The ideal velocity of sound is 330 ms–1]
Solution:
f = 512 Hz
\[{\ell _1} = 30.7\;cm\]
\[{\ell _2} = 63.2\;cm\]
\[\lambda = 2\left( {{\ell _2} - {\ell _1}} \right)\]
\[\lambda = 2\left[ {63.2 - 30.7} \right]\]
\[v = f\lambda \]
v = 512 × 0.65 m
v = 332.8 m/s
∆v = 332.8 – 330 = 2.8 m/s = 280 cm/s

Question: A liquid of specific heat $0.9\text{cal}/{{\text{g}}^{\circ }}\text{C}$ at a temperature of ${{80}^{\circ }}\text{C}$ is mixed with another liquid of the same mass initially at ${{30}^{\circ }}\text{C}$. If the steady-state temperature of the mixture at thermal equilibrium is ${{60}^{\circ }}\text{C}$ (assuming no heat loss to the surroundings), the specific heat of the second liquid (in $\text{cal}/{{\text{g}}^{\circ }}\text{C}$ ) is:
Solution: By the principle of calorimetry, Heat Lost $=$ Heat Gained. Therefore, $m{{s}_{1}}\left( {{T}_{1}}-{{T}_{f}} \right)=m{{s}_{2}}\left( {{T}_{f}}-{{T}_{2}} \right)$. Substituting the given values: $m\left( 0.9 \right)\left( 80-60 \right)=m\left( {{s}_{2}} \right)\left( 60-30 \right)$. Simplifying yields $0.9\left( 20 \right)={{s}_{2}}\left( 30 \right)\Rightarrow 18=30{{s}_{2}}\Rightarrow {{s}_{2}}=\frac{18}{30}=0.6\text{cal}/{{\text{g}}^{\circ }}\text{C}$.

Question: Which of the following tests can be used to distinguish Phenol from carboxylic acid?

Options a and b are incorrect because both phenols and carboxylic acids react with sodium metal and sodium hydroxide, making them indistinguishable by those methods.
